text:
The lume CLI is the agent's interface to the loop - an agent dropped cold into
a repo drives lume entirely through its verbs. MCP (the Model Context Protocol)
is a successful, widely-adopted standard for exposing capabilities to agents,
and its structure encodes hard-won principles: capability discovery, typed
schemas per operation, self-description, predictable verb/argument grammar,
machine-parseable I/O, and explicit, structured errors.

lume will NOT adopt MCP or expose an MCP server - that would breach the
dependency-free / stdlib-only non-negotiable, and lume's interface is a CLI,
not a protocol server. The value is in the PRINCIPLES, not the protocol. This
workstream investigates how MCP structures an agent-friendly interface,
distils the transferable principles, and applies the ones that fit to the lume
CLI so it is well-structured and genuinely agent-friendly: discoverable,
self-describing, consistent, and machine-parseable - building on the discovery
verbs lume already has (entities / schema / get).

Non-negotiables carried in: dependency-free (stdlib only); deterministic over
inference (no LLM in any verb, gate, or output); reuse before adding; ceremony
must buy back its cost.

Done-when: MCP's agent-interface principles are distilled into a concrete,
lume-specific checklist; the current CLI is assessed against it; and the gaps
that matter are closed in the CLI (e.g. consistent verb/flag grammar,
machine-readable structured output and errors, complete discovery/schema
coverage) - with no new dependency and no LLM in the path. The concrete set of
principles to adopt vs reject, and which CLI changes they imply, is a
discovery/planning decision; discovery may conclude that only a subset is worth
applying.
title: Agent-Friendly CLI (MCP-informed)
